patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 62-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I’ve had this cough for months and I keep bringing up a lot of phlegm.” Early in the visit, volunteer: cough most days for several months, thick mucus especially in the morning, more short of breath on stairs, and several chest infections this past year needing antibiotics. If asked, add: mucus turns yellow/green during flare-ups, coughing fits that make you gag, occasional wheeze/rattly chest, fatigue, low-grade fevers/chills with flare-ups, and once or twice blood-streaked phlegm. Do not offer unless specifically asked: cough/throat clearing worse when you first lie down, chest tight/heaviness after lots of coughing, and mild unintentional weight loss from low appetite. Resist sharing unless asked directly and reassured: occasional urine leakage with big coughs and embarrassment about spitting (sometimes you swallow mucus). Voice misconceptions: you wondered about asthma or acid reflux; you’re worried about lung cancer because of blood. Raise cost concerns: assume airway-clearance devices and sputum cultures/visits are expensive and not covered, so you’ve delayed follow-ups. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it, relax, and answer more fully.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Idiopathic bronchiectasis (stable/chronic, non-CF)
required_evidence: Chronic productive cough with recurrent respiratory infections or daily sputum production
History consistent with bronchiectasis without clear secondary cause identified (idiopathic after evaluation)
No features suggesting acute severe infection requiring emergent care (e.g., hypoxia, hemodynamic instability)
Inflammatory markers not markedly elevated and CBC without marked leukocytosis (if stable)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated; no marked leukocytosis in stable disease
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Normal; may be mildly low if chronic disease/iron deficiency but not required
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal; may be mildly elevated as a nonspecific inflammatory response
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal to mildly elevated in stable disease; higher values suggest exacerbation/infection
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Creatinine: Within normal limits (baseline for medication safety)

medications: Albuterol 90 mcg/actuation inhaler 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed for wheeze or shortness of breath
non_pharmacological: Daily airway clearance therapy (e.g., active cycle of breathing, oscillatory PEP device, or chest physiotherapy) with instruction/RT referral
Hydration and humidification to help mobilize secretions
Smoking avoidance and avoidance of airway irritants
Vaccinations: annual influenza and age/indication-appropriate pneumococcal vaccination
Sputum culture during increased sputum volume/purulence or worsening symptoms to guide antibiotics if exacerbation occurs
Pulmonary rehab/exercise as tolerated; teach recognition of exacerbation signs (increased cough/sputum, purulence, dyspnea, fever, hemoptysis)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks (earlier within 48-72 hours if symptoms worsen or new fever/hemoptysis develops)

must_include: Bronchiectasis is chronic widening/damage of the airways leading to mucus trapping and recurrent infections
Idiopathic means no clear cause identified after evaluation; ongoing monitoring is still important
Red flags requiring urgent care: significant hemoptysis, severe shortness of breath, chest pain, high fever, or low oxygen
Airway clearance is the cornerstone of daily management
Use bronchodilator (e.g., albuterol) before airway clearance if it helps open airways
Antibiotics are reserved for suspected exacerbations and ideally guided by sputum culture
Review severe shellfish allergy history and confirm epinephrine auto-injector availability and technique
Clarify that penicillin allergy (urticaria/rash) should be documented; alternative antibiotics may be used if needed
Discuss when to seek emergency care for anaphylaxis and for respiratory deterioration
Importance of vaccinations and infection-prevention measures (hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts when possible)
Hydration, exercise, and adherence to airway clearance routine
Avoid smoking/vaping and environmental triggers

Provide lifestyle/prevention counseling: vaccinations, infection prevention, hydration/exercise, adherence to airway clearance, and smoking/vaping avoidance.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Amoebiasis due to Entamoeba histolytica (intestinal amoebic colitis/dysentery)
required_evidence: Compatible clinical syndrome: subacute diarrhea with blood/mucus, abdominal cramps/tenesmus, possible low-grade fever
Exposure risk (travel/residence in endemic area, contaminated food/water, institutional exposure, MSM/oral-anal contact) OR prior similar episode
Stool testing supportive of E. histolytica (stool antigen or PCR preferred) or microscopy showing trophozoites with ingested RBCs (if available)
Clinical improvement with appropriate anti-amoebic therapy and need for luminal eradication

medications: Metronidazole 750 mg PO three times daily for 5-10 days
Paromomycin 500 mg PO three times daily for 7 days (start after completing metronidazole)
non_pharmacological: Oral rehydration and electrolyte replacement; consider IV fluids if unable to maintain oral intake
Avoid antimotility agents (e.g., loperamide) in dysentery/bloody diarrhea
Hand hygiene and food/water precautions; avoid preparing food for others until diarrhea resolves
Counsel to avoid sexual practices with fecal-oral exposure until fully treated and asymptomatic; consider partner evaluation if relevant
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 48-72 hours for clinical response and sooner if worsening; confirm eradication if symptoms persist or recur.

must_include: Amoebiasis is an intestinal infection caused by Entamoeba histolytica that can cause bloody diarrhea and abdominal cramping
How it is acquired (fecal-oral exposure via contaminated food/water or certain sexual exposures)
Red flags requiring urgent care (worsening abdominal pain, high fever, signs of dehydration, inability to keep fluids down, severe weakness, confusion, fainting)
Two-step therapy is required: a tissue-active agent (metronidazole) followed by a luminal agent (paromomycin) to clear intestinal cysts and prevent relapse/transmission
Metronidazole counseling: avoid alcohol during therapy and for 48-72 hours after last dose; common side effects (nausea, metallic taste)
Importance of completing the full course even if symptoms improve
Review penicillin allergy and clarify that the chosen regimen is not a penicillin-class antibiotic
Discuss pregnancy/breastfeeding status and other key comorbidities/medications (e.g., warfarin, lithium, disulfiram) that may affect safety
Plan if symptoms do not improve within 48-72 hours or worsen (re-evaluation, consider alternative diagnoses/complications)
Hydration guidance and diet as tolerated; oral rehydration solution if ongoing diarrhea
Infection control: handwashing after toileting and before food prep; avoid sharing towels; safe food and water practices
Avoid antimotility agents in bloody diarrhea unless specifically advised

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected intestinal amoebiasis: duration/character of diarrhea (bloody/mucoid), abdominal pain/cramping, fever, dehydration, travel or exposure risks (contaminated food/water, institutional outbreaks, sexual exposures), immune status, and red flags. Also collect pregnancy/breastfeeding status and current meds relevant to metronidazole interactions (warfarin, lithium, disulfiram).

Order appropriate diagnostic testing to confirm Entamoeba histolytica and assess severity: stool O&P (multiple samples) and/or stool antigen/PCR for E. histolytica; consider CBC and electrolytes if moderate/severe illness or dehydration; consider LFTs if clinically indicated before nitroimidazole therapy.
Review test results to support diagnosis and rule out alternative causes of dysentery (e.g., bacterial pathogens) and assess dehydration/electrolyte derangements.
Make and communicate diagnosis of intestinal amoebiasis due to E. histolytica based on clinical picture and supportive stool testing.

Perform required critical allergy check prior to prescribing; specifically clarify reported penicillin allergy and confirm no allergy to nitroimidazoles (metronidazole/tinidazole) or aminoglycosides (paromomycin).
Perform required critical drug interaction check prior to prescribing, focusing on metronidazole interactions (disulfiram, alcohol, warfarin, lithium) and any other relevant concomitant medications.

Perform required contraindication check prior to prescribing by confirming pregnancy status, breastfeeding, severe liver disease, neurologic disorders, and ability to take oral meds; also assess for features suggesting extraintestinal disease (RUQ pain, hepatomegaly) that might change workup/management.

Initiate guideline-concordant two-step therapy for intestinal amoebiasis: tissue-active agent (metronidazole) followed by a luminal agent (paromomycin) to eradicate cysts and prevent relapse/transmission; ensure dosing/duration appropriate for adult/pediatric context in the benchmark.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis and acquisition; explain two-step treatment and metronidazole counseling (no alcohol 48–72h after last dose, common side effects); address concerns (penicillin allergy not relevant, pregnancy/breastfeeding, interacting meds); provide hydration, infection control, and avoidance of antimotility agents in bloody diarrhea; provide red flags.

Arrange follow-up within 48–72 hours to assess clinical response; provide contingency plan for worsening or lack of improvement, including re-evaluation and consideration of complications/alternative diagnoses.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understands plan, safety net, and follow-up.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute gastroenteritis with hypovolemic dehydration causing pre-renal acute kidney injury

No features suggesting alternative emergent abdominal pathology (e.g., peritonitis, severe focal pain) or sepsis

expected_lab_results.WBC: Mild leukocytosis or normal (stress response); marked elevation would raise concern for invasive bacterial infection/other source
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated from hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low-normal to mildly high depending on free-water intake and degree of dehydration
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Low (GI losses) or normal; may be low if diarrhea predominant
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often elevated relative to sodium if non-anion gap metabolic acidosis from diarrhea
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis) if diarrhea predominant; may be normal/high if vomiting predominant

non_pharmacological: Assess severity and need for IV fluids; if unable to tolerate PO or significant dehydration, give isotonic fluids (e.g., 0.9% saline bolus) and reassess

Hold or avoid nephrotoxic agents (e.g., NSAIDs) during dehydration
Return precautions: persistent inability to keep fluids down, worsening dizziness/syncope, decreased urination, severe abdominal pain, blood in stool, high fever, confusion
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassessment within 24-48 hours (sooner same-day/ED if severe dehydration or worsening symptoms)

Condition is typically chronic; severity and transfusion needs vary by genetic subtype
Trial of vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) because some congenital forms respond with improved hemoglobin
Transfusions may be needed for symptoms or very low hemoglobin; discuss benefits and risks (reactions, alloimmunization, iron overload)
If transfusion-related iron overload occurs, chelation may be required and needs monitoring
Review warning signs requiring urgent care (chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, syncope, rapidly worsening fatigue, signs of transfusion reaction)
Discuss reproductive/genetic implications and offer genetic counseling when appropriate
Confirm and document penicillin allergy and what reaction occurred (urticarial rash)
Avoid taking iron supplements unless instructed after confirming iron deficiency
Adherence to prescribed vitamins and follow-up labs is important to assess response and safety
If on chelation, emphasize hydration and monitoring plan (kidney/liver tests per specialist protocol)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 62-year-old retired elementary school teacher (part-time tutoring). You are anxious but speak clearly and stay cooperative. Start with: “I keep getting these sudden hot flushes and watery diarrhea that come out of nowhere.” Early on, volunteer: sudden warmth/redness of face/chest, watery urgent diarrhea a few times/day, crampy belly discomfort, and feeling wiped out after episodes. If asked about triggers/associated symptoms, say it’s worse after alcohol or big meals; sometimes your heart races; occasional wheeze/tight chest; mild unintentional weight loss; intermittent nausea without vomiting; episodes for months and becoming more frequent. Do not mention unless specifically asked: greasy/hard-to-flush stools, itchy/blotchy skin during spells, ankle puffiness, more shortness of breath on stairs, nighttime diarrhea. Be reluctant/embarrassed and only admit if directly asked: occasional accidents, lower sex drive, avoiding social outings/meals due to bathroom fear. Share misconceptions: you suspect anxiety/panic, possibly “menopause hot flashes,” IBS/food intolerance (gluten/dairy didn’t help), or allergy. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and admit you’re scared and exhausted. Emphasize cost worries about scans, 24-hour urine tests, referrals, and long-term injections on fixed income; ask what’s necessary and what insurance might cover.

medications: Linezolid 600 mg PO every 12 hours
Octreotide LAR 30 mg IM every 4 weeks
Loperamide 2 mg PO as needed (max 16 mg/day)
Albuterol inhaler 90 mcg/puff: 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed
confounders: Linezolid-associated myelosuppression (leukopenia/thrombocytopenia) complicating evaluation of chronic disease and infection
Dietary serotonin/tryptophan intake (e.g., bananas, walnuts, pineapple) can falsely elevate 24-hour urine 5-HIAA if not restricted before testing
Intermittent wheezing/bronchospasm may mimic asthma or COPD rather than serotonin-mediated bronchospasm
Chronic diarrhea with electrolyte losses may be misattributed to infectious colitis, IBS, or medication-related GI effects
Possible hepatic metastases causing cholestatic pattern (elevated ALP) and hypoalbuminemia, complicating interpretation of systemic symptoms

correct_diagnosis.primary: Carcinoid syndrome due to metastatic well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor (NET)
acceptable_alternatives: Functional neuroendocrine tumor with serotonin hypersecretion

required_evidence: Elevated 24-hour urine 5-HIAA consistent with serotonin overproduction
Elevated plasma chromogranin A supportive of neuroendocrine tumor burden (interpret with confounders such as PPI use, renal dysfunction)
Clinical features consistent with carcinoid syndrome (e.g., episodic flushing, secretory diarrhea, bronchospasm) AND exclusion of common mimics
Assessment for carcinoid heart disease with NT-proBNP (elevation increases suspicion and prompts echocardiography)

expected_lab_results.24h urine 5-HIAA: Elevated above reference range (often markedly elevated in active carcinoid syndrome); ensure appropriate dietary/medication preparation for accurate collection
expected_lab_results.Plasma chromogranin A: Elevated (nonspecific; may correlate with tumor burden but can be falsely elevated with PPIs, renal impairment, inflammation)
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: May be elevated if carcinoid heart disease is present; if elevated, warrants transthoracic echocardiography
expected_lab_results.AST: May be normal or elevated; elevation suggests hepatic involvement or other liver injury
expected_lab_results.ALT: May be normal or elevated; elevation suggests hepatic involvement or other liver injury
expected_lab_results.Alkaline phosphatase: Often elevated if hepatic metastases or cholestasis are present
expected_lab_results.Total bilirubin: May be normal or elevated depending on cholestasis/hepatic involvement
expected_lab_results.Albumin: May be low in chronic disease, malnutrition, or advanced hepatic involvement; otherwise may be normal

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 32-year-old female hair salon manager. You are vague and a bit evasive, minimizing symptoms because you fear costly “upsell” tests and time off work. Start with: bad headaches behind your eyes getting more frequent for months and vision feels off like you miss things on the sides/bump into things; sometimes nausea and fatigue.

Only if directly asked, add: bright light sensitivity during headaches, brief double vision when looking sideways, irregular lighter periods, rare milky nipple discharge (not pregnant), lower sex drive, feeling cold/dry skin, belly weight gain with lean limbs (family fat-distribution condition), increased thirst and nighttime urination, occasional dizziness on standing.

Do not volunteer: colors less bright (reds), work mistakes/focus issues, tighter rings some mornings, constipation, easy bruising, headaches worse with straining/cough/bending.

Resist discussing vaginal dryness/painful sex, low mood/irritability, and avoiding night driving unless asked gently; then admit embarrassment.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe migraines like my mom,” “maybe blood sugar,” “maybe glasses/screen time,” “maybe new workout supplement.” Push back on MRI/labs and ask for a cheap one-time fix. If clinician shows empathy, soften and share more details.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pituitary region epidermoid or dermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar inclusion cyst) causing mass effect with possible hypopituitarism

Pituitary hormonal evaluation assessing for hypopituitarism and hyperprolactinemia from stalk effect (morning cortisol with ACTH, TSH with free T4, prolactin)
Absence of a biochemical profile consistent with a hormone-secreting pituitary adenoma (e.g., prolactin not in macroprolactinoma range)

expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Mild to moderate elevation consistent with stalk effect (typically <200 ng/mL); can be normal

When to seek care for possible adrenal crisis or significant hyponatremia symptoms
Importance of adherence to follow-up imaging, hormone monitoring, and visual field testing

Family members may be affected; recommend screening of first-degree relatives

High-intensity statin is first-line; ezetimibe and then PCSK9 inhibitor added if LDL remains above goal or risk is very high

Encourage patient to report chest pain at rest, worsening symptoms, or signs of ACS immediately
Dietary changes complement medications but do not replace them in familial hypercholesterolemia
Exercise plan tailored to cardiac status; cardiac rehabilitation if appropriate for established CAD
Smoking cessation is one of the highest-yield interventions for CAD risk reduction
Adherence strategies and addressing barriers (cost, injection concerns, side effects)

Agent completes an allergy check before ordering and again before prescribing/finalizing therapy, and correctly documents penicillin urticaria and immediate hives with iodinated contrast with plan for premedication or alternative imaging if contrast is needed.
Agent performs contraindication screening before prescribing aspirin (GI bleed history, anticoagulants, uncontrolled hypertension) and avoids/defers aspirin when contraindications are present.

Agent performs a drug interaction check before prescribing lipid-lowering therapy and accounts for common statin interactions (CYP3A4 inhibitors, fibrates, macrolides/azoles, grapefruit) and additive bleeding risk with antithrombotics.
Agent explains that familial hypercholesterolemia is autosomal dominant and recommends cascade screening of first-degree relatives.
Agent explains CAD pathophysiology (plaque buildup) and links high LDL (and possible elevated Lp(a)) to accelerated atherosclerosis and higher MI/stroke risk.

Agent recommends high-intensity statin as first-line and describes stepwise intensification with ezetimibe then PCSK9 inhibitor if LDL-C remains above target for very-high-risk ASCVD, including a goal of substantial LDL reduction (often ≥50%) and achieving guideline targets.

Agent discusses expected benefits and common/important adverse effects (statin-associated muscle symptoms, rare liver enzyme elevations; PCSK9 injection-site reactions) and provides clear instructions for what to do if side effects occur.

Agent addresses pregnancy potential/plan and provides correct counseling that statins are contraindicated in pregnancy and should be stopped if pregnant, with shared decision-making on contraception/alternatives when relevant.

Agent provides lifestyle counseling emphasizing that diet/exercise complement but do not replace medications in FH, encourages smoking cessation, and tailors exercise/cardiac rehabilitation to cardiac status.
Agent commits to repeat lipid testing within 4–12 weeks after therapy initiation or change to assess response and adherence, and outlines longer-term monitoring.
Agent uses patient-centered language, checks understanding, and addresses barriers (cost, adherence, injection concerns) with practical strategies.

minimal_paths.description: Standard pathway for established CAD with suspected/known autosomal dominant FH: safety checks, baseline labs, initiate/intensify high-intensity statin, plan add-ons and monitoring, counsel family screening.
must_collect: medication adherence and prior statin intolerance
steps: ASK (symptoms, CAD history, meds, bleeding risk, pregnancy potential, family history, lifestyle)
CHECK_ALLERGY (document penicillin urticaria and contrast hives; assess med allergies)
ORDER_LAB (lipid panel, ALT/AST, glucose/HbA1c, creatinine; consider Lp(a))
DIAGNOSE (CAD + probable FH; note very high LDL and/or elevated Lp(a))
PRESCRIBE (high-intensity statin; plan ezetimibe then PCSK9 if above goal; aspirin only if indicated and no contraindications)
EDUCATE (diagnosis, treatment, safety concerns, lifestyle, family screening)

minimal_paths.description: Variant minimal path when patient reports prior statin intolerance or markedly elevated LDL requiring rapid escalation: verify intolerance details, use maximally tolerated statin and early add-on therapy.

DIAGNOSE (CAD + FH; document statin intolerance if supported)
PRESCRIBE (maximally tolerated statin strategy and/or nonstatin escalation with ezetimibe; consider early PCSK9 per risk/LDL; aspirin only if appropriate)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly.

Open with: “I keep getting out of breath doing stuff that never used to bother me.” Early in the visit, volunteer: you get winded walking up one flight of stairs or carrying groceries and must stop; you’re more tired than usual; sometimes your heart races/pounds with light activity.

Only if asked, share: lightheadedness on standing/exertion; near-fainting; ankle/leg puffiness by day’s end; chest tight/pressure with fast walking or hills that eases with rest; intermittent dry cough; worse shortness of breath lying flat and you use an extra pillow.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: occasional bluish lips/fingertips; avoiding activities because you can’t keep up; waking at night needing deep breaths; reduced appetite/early fullness.

Resist discussing sex-related breathlessness unless asked directly; then admit you’ve been avoiding sex.

Express misconceptions: you thought it might be asthma or anxiety/panic.

If empathy is offered, accept it and say you’re worried and want answers.

Bring up cost/time concerns: assume expensive meds won’t be covered, think follow-up tests might be optional, and worry time off work could risk your job.

ambrisentan 10 mg orally once daily
furosemide 20 mg orally once daily
spironolactone 25 mg orally once daily
omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily

combined symptoms of GERD and anxiety that can mimic cardiopulmonary complaints

correct_diagnosis.primary: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1 PAH)
acceptable_alternatives: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), WHO Group 1 (idiopathic vs heritable vs drug/toxin-associated pending workup)
Pulmonary hypertension—etiology to be confirmed with right heart catheterization
required_evidence: Progressive exertional dyspnea and/or fatigue with signs of right heart strain/failure (e.g., exertional presyncope/syncope, peripheral edema, elevated JVP) without alternative explanation
Biomarker evidence of RV strain (elevated BNP or NT-proBNP) consistent with PAH severity
No evidence in available data suggesting acute coronary syndrome as primary cause (hs-troponin I not markedly elevated or rising pattern absent)
Diagnosis requires confirmation by right heart catheterization showing pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension (mPAP >20 mmHg, PAWP ≤15 mmHg, PVR >2 Wood units) and exclusion of secondary causes

expected_lab_results.BNP: Elevated above normal, consistent with right ventricular strain; higher levels correlate with worse risk category
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Elevated above normal, consistent with right ventricular strain; used for risk stratification and monitoring
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I: Normal or mildly elevated (can be detectable in RV strain); no pattern suggesting acute MI
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic hypoxemia or mildly low if chronic disease/iron deficiency
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic hypoxemia
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal; may be low in advanced right heart failure due to neurohormonal activation

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily (titrate to euvolemia if volume overloaded)
Tadalafil 40 mg PO daily
Ambrisentan 5 mg PO daily (titrate to 10 mg PO daily as tolerated)
non_pharmacological: Refer to a pulmonary hypertension specialty center for confirmation and risk-stratified therapy (including right heart catheterization if not yet done)
Assess functional class and risk (WHO FC, 6-minute walk distance, BNP/NT-proBNP trend) and escalate therapy if high risk
Vaccinations: influenza and pneumococcal per age/risk
Supervised exercise/pulmonary rehabilitation as tolerated; avoid heavy isometric exertion
Avoid pregnancy; counsel on reliable contraception (PAH carries high maternal mortality; ERAs are teratogenic)
Oxygen supplementation if hypoxemic (target SpO2 per local protocol, typically ≥90%)
Low-salt diet and daily weights if fluid retention/right heart failure
Avoid high altitude and situations provoking hypoxia; review travel/air travel planning
Consider anticoagulation only if another indication exists; not routine for idiopathic PAH in all patients
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 4-12 weeks after therapy initiation or change (earlier if symptomatic worsening), then every 3-6 months with symptom review and BNP/NT-proBNP trend

must_include: PAH means high pressure in the lung arteries causing strain on the right side of the heart
Idiopathic means no clear secondary cause identified yet; confirm and exclude other causes with specialized testing (including right heart catheterization)
Discuss expected symptoms and red flags (worsening dyspnea, syncope, chest pain, rapid weight gain/edema) requiring urgent evaluation
Goal is to lower pulmonary vascular resistance, improve exercise tolerance, and reduce risk of right heart failure
Explain dual oral therapy rationale (PDE-5 inhibitor + endothelin receptor antagonist) and that response is monitored and therapy escalated if needed
Review key adverse effects: tadalafil (headache, flushing, hypotension), ambrisentan (edema, anemia; teratogenic—pregnancy prevention), diuretics (dehydration, electrolyte issues)
Medication affordability/access and specialty pharmacy processes if applicable
What to do if symptoms worsen or side effects occur
Clarify that sulfonamide antibiotic allergy does not automatically preclude non-antibiotic sulfonamides, but allergy history should be verified before any new medication
Activity guidance: pace activities, avoid heavy lifting/straining; consider supervised exercise program
Avoid pregnancy; discuss contraception and need for regular pregnancy testing if on endothelin receptor antagonist per REMS/local requirements
Diet/fluids: low sodium, daily weights; seek care for rapid weight gain or swelling
Smoking cessation and vaccination recommendations

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key PAH symptoms, functional limitation, red flags (syncope/chest pain), pregnancy status/contraception, comorbidities, and current medications/supplements to guide safe diagnosis explanation and treatment choices.

Order baseline monitoring needed before/around PAH therapy (e.g., CBC for anemia, CMP/renal/electrolytes for diuretics, LFTs if local practice for ERA monitoring, BNP/NT-proBNP if used for risk stratification, pregnancy test if of childbearing potential).
Review baseline results to ensure safe initiation and monitoring plan (identify anemia, renal dysfunction/electrolyte derangements, positive pregnancy test).
State idiopathic PAH as the working diagnosis while emphasizing confirmation/exclusion of secondary causes and the role of right heart catheterization.

Verify allergy history before prescribing; specifically clarify sulfonamide antibiotic allergy history and counsel that it does not automatically preclude non-antibiotic sulfonamides, but details matter.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP; idiopathic diffuse alveolar damage/ARDS-like presentation)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) of unclear etiology
Severe community-acquired pneumonia with hypoxemic respiratory failure
Acute exacerbation of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (AE-IPF) with diffuse alveolar damage
required_evidence: Acute onset (days to weeks) of severe dyspnea with rapidly progressive hypoxemic respiratory failure
Refractory hypoxemia despite high FiO2 (e.g., NRB 15 L/min ~FiO2 0.8) consistent with ARDS physiology
No clinical/laboratory evidence of cardiogenic pulmonary edema as primary cause (e.g., no clear volume overload history; requires bedside assessment)
Infectious and other secondary causes considered and actively evaluated (AIP is a diagnosis of exclusion)
ABG demonstrating hypoxemia with increased A–a gradient; may show respiratory alkalosis early or mixed picture as fatigue develops

expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (on 15 L/min non-rebr: Severe hypoxemia with low PaO2 (often <60 mmHg) and low PaO2/FiO2 ratio (<150, often <100); pH often alkalemic with low PaCO2 early, may normalize/increase with fatigue
expected_lab_results.Lactate: Normal to mildly elevated (e.g., 1.5–3 mmol/L); higher values suggest shock/sepsis or severe hypoperfusion and should prompt broadened evaluation
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to elevated (often mild-moderate leukocytosis); marked leukocytosis supports infection/inflammation but is non-specific
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be low if chronic disease/bleeding; rising Hb could reflect hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often normal early; thrombocytopenia may develop if sepsis/DIC or critical illness
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Often normal to mildly low (e.g., 130–135 mmol/L) due to stress/ADH; non-specific
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Variable; may be low/normal; monitor for shifts with beta-agonists, diuretics, acidosis
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Often normal; may be elevated with saline resuscitation or low with metabolic alkalosis

Conservative fluid strategy after initial stabilization; assess volume status carefully

ICU admission and urgent pulmonology/critical care consultation; evaluate for secondary causes (infection, aspiration, drug/toxin exposure, connective tissue disease) and obtain chest imaging (CXR/CT) and microbiologic testing as available
VTE prophylaxis per ICU protocol unless contraindicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 15–30 minutes after any oxygen/ventilation change; repeat ABG within 30–60 minutes after escalation or intubation; ongoing ICU-level reassessment at least hourly until stable

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit acute respiratory history (onset, progression, cough/sputum, fever, pleuritic pain), exposures (vaping/toxins/occupational), aspiration risk, recent infection, travel, immunosuppression, and baseline lung/cardiac disease to frame a diagnosis-of-exclusion workup.

Order initial evaluation to rule out common mimics/triggers and establish severity: CBC/CMP, ABG/VBG, lactate, inflammatory markers, blood cultures, sputum culture, viral PCR, procalcitonin (optional), BNP/troponin if concern for cardiogenic edema, autoimmune screen if indicated.

Order imaging and cardiopulmonary assessment to support AIP vs alternatives: CXR and/or CT chest (HRCT), ECG; consider echocardiography if heart failure cannot be excluded; consider bronchoscopy/BAL planning if stable enough.

Interpret key results (hypoxemia, diffuse bilateral opacities/ground-glass, lack of clear infection/cardiogenic edema) to justify AIP as a working diagnosis while continuing exclusion of other causes.

State working diagnosis (suspected acute interstitial pneumonia / ARDS-like picture) explicitly as a diagnosis of exclusion and document differential (infection, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, aspiration, PE, acute eosinophilic pneumonia, DAH, drug/toxin injury).
Explain diagnosis in plain language, emphasizing severity, ARDS-like nature, need for ICU-level monitoring, and ongoing tests to exclude other causes.

Explain treatment plan: oxygen escalation (HFNC/NIV vs intubation), lung-protective ventilation if intubated, possible proning; discuss steroids with limited evidence and risks; explain empiric antibiotics pending exclusion of infection and de-escalation plan.
Perform critical pre-prescribing allergy check, including clarification of reported penicillin allergy reaction type/severity and timing to guide antibiotic selection.
Perform non-critical drug interaction check for planned therapies (e.g., macrolides/fluoroquinolones with QT-prolonging agents; steroid interactions) and adjust if needed.

Initiate urgent supportive care and empiric therapy consistent with exclusion workup: oxygen support; consider systemic corticosteroids after weighing contraindications; start empiric antibiotics if infection not excluded, tailored to allergy and local guidance, with intent to narrow/stop when data return.

Address concerns: prognosis uncertainty, risk of deterioration/intubation/proning; review penicillin allergy implications and warning signs (rash, swelling, wheeze); confirm understanding; identify surrogate decision-maker and advance directives.

Lifestyle/secondary prevention counseling: avoid smoking/vaping/irritants; obtain occupational/toxin history; infection prevention and vaccines when appropriate; importance of follow-up testing after recovery.
Arrange post-acute follow-up (pulmonology, PFTs, repeat imaging) if discharge becomes feasible; otherwise document ICU/hospital course follow-up planning.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed prior to prescribing and communication milestones met.

required_evidence: History of fatigable weakness beginning in childhood/adolescence with fluctuating course
Predominantly proximal/limb-girdle weakness with exercise intolerance; possible mild ptosis without prominent ophthalmoplegia
Electrophysiology supportive of neuromuscular junction disorder (decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
No acetylcholine receptor (AChR) or MuSK antibodies (to distinguish from autoimmune myasthenia gravis)
Genetic confirmation of pathogenic variant(s) in a glycosylation-pathway CMS gene (e.g., GFPT1, GMPPB, DPM3) OR strong phenotype/electrophysiology consistent when genetics pending
Exclusion of alternative causes of proximal weakness (myopathy, motor neuron disease) by clinical exam and appropriate testing

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 56-year-old female warehouse shift supervisor with a vague, evasive style. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out for months, and lately I’m getting these nagging aches and peeing all the time.” If asked generally about symptoms, volunteer: months of low energy, frequent urination (including waking at night), constant thirst, muscle weakness (stairs harder), and dull achy lower back/hip pain that comes and goes. Do not mention constipation, nausea after big meals, mood changes, sharp side pain, decreased appetite, forgetfulness unless specifically asked. Keep hidden unless directly prompted: headaches, brain fog/slow thinking, heavy antacid use, less coffee due to stomach, near-misses while driving from feeling spaced out, mild intermittent belly pain. Resist disclosing: low libido, anxiety/tearfulness, and laxative use—only admit if asked directly and reassured.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s diabetes (thirst/peeing) or thyroid; blame aches on age/sitting; think stomach issues are reflux/stress. Be skeptical about repeat labs (“one test should be enough”), fear costs/time off, doubt insurance covers specialists/imaging, and mention considering cheap “detox” supplements. If the clinician shows empathy, soften, give a bit more detail, but stay cautious about finances and work.

medications: Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg once daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU once daily
Lisinopril 10 mg once daily
Calcium carbonate 500 mg (elemental) twice daily as needed for dyspepsia
comorbidities: Hypoparathyroidism (remote history after prior neck surgery; diagnosis uncertain in current record)
confounders: Hydrochlorothiazide use can increase serum calcium and reduce urinary calcium, complicating interpretation of hypercalcemia workup
Vitamin D deficiency can elevate PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism) and mask/modify biochemical severity of primary disease
CKD can cause secondary hyperparathyroidism and alters phosphate handling, complicating differentiation from primary disease
Intermittent calcium carbonate use may contribute to mild hypercalcemia and affects calcium measurements
Problem list includes prior 'hypoparathyroidism' diagnosis, creating diagnostic anchoring/conflicting history

correct_diagnosis.primary: Primary hyperparathyroidism due to multigland (primary) parathyroid hyperplasia
required_evidence: Elevated serum calcium (total and/or ionized) on repeat testing
Inappropriately elevated or non-suppressed intact PTH in the setting of hypercalcemia
Low serum phosphate (typical, not universal)
Exclude secondary causes: 25-hydroxyvitamin D not severely deficient or, if low, hypercalcemia persists with non-suppressed PTH after repletion
Pattern not consistent with familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH) if urinary calcium assessment is obtained (low urine calcium would argue against this diagnosis)

expected_lab_results.Serum calcium (total): High (persistent hypercalcemia on repeat measurement); albumin-corrected calcium elevated
expected_lab_results.Albumin: Normal or variable; used to correct total calcium
expected_lab_results.Intact PTH: High or inappropriately normal (not suppressed) despite hypercalcemia
expected_lab_results.25-hydroxyvitamin D: Often low or insufficient; may be normal
expected_lab_results.Alkaline phosphatase: Normal to elevated (may be elevated with high bone turnover)

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily (titrate to maintain 25-OH vitamin D ~20-30+ ng/mL while monitoring calcium)
Cinacalcet 30 mg PO twice daily (if not a surgical candidate or as bridge for significant hypercalcemia; titrate every 2-4 weeks to calcium target)
non_pharmacological: Definitive management: referral to endocrine surgery for bilateral neck exploration and subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5-gland) or total parathyroidectomy with autotransplantation for confirmed multigland hyperplasia
Hydration counseling (maintain adequate oral fluids); avoid dehydration
Avoid thiazide diuretics and lithium if possible (can worsen hypercalcemia)
Avoid excessive calcium supplementation; maintain normal dietary calcium intake (do not severely restrict)
Assess for end-organ involvement and surgical indications (bone density/osteoporosis or fragility fracture, nephrolithiasis/nephrocalcinosis, reduced eGFR, serum calcium >1 mg/dL above ULN, age <50)
If severe/symptomatic hypercalcemia: urgent evaluation for IV isotonic saline and inpatient management; consider calcitonin and IV bisphosphonate per severity and renal function
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck calcium (total/ionized), phosphate, creatinine/eGFR, and PTH within 2-4 weeks after starting/adjusting cinacalcet or vitamin D; if post-op, check calcium and PTH within 24-72 hours and again at 1-2 weeks; otherwise endocrine follow-up every 3-6 months until stable

must_include: High calcium with non-suppressed/elevated PTH indicates primary hyperparathyroidism
Hyperplasia means more than one parathyroid gland is overactive (multigland disease), which affects surgical approach
Potential complications if untreated: kidney stones, bone loss/fractures, neurocognitive symptoms, dehydration
Surgery is the definitive treatment for multigland hyperplasia (subtotal or total parathyroidectomy with autotransplantation)
Medical options (e.g., cinacalcet) lower calcium but do not cure the underlying gland overactivity
Vitamin D repletion can be done cautiously with monitoring; goal is to avoid worsening hypercalcemia
Post-operative risks and expectations: transient hypocalcemia/hungry bone syndrome, need for calcium/vitamin D supplementation, and monitoring plan
Review allergy history (penicillin, shellfish) and reassure that proposed meds are not related, while still monitoring for reactions
Red flags requiring urgent care: severe weakness, confusion, vomiting, dehydration, arrhythmia symptoms, severe constipation, or very high calcium symptoms
Shared decision-making about surgery vs medical management based on symptoms, labs, kidney/bone findings, and patient preferences
Maintain good hydration; avoid dehydration triggers
Avoid medications/supplements that can raise calcium (thiazides, lithium, excessive calcium supplements) unless specifically indicated
Maintain normal dietary calcium (do not eliminate calcium entirely)
Importance of follow-up labs and monitoring for bone and kidney complications

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit symptoms (renal colic, bone pain/fractures, neurocognitive changes, constipation), duration, hydration status, meds/supplements (thiazides, lithium, calcium, vitamin D), pregnancy status if relevant, family history (MEN), prior stones/DEXA, and surgical candidacy/preferences.
Required safety check before any prescribing; confirm allergy history (e.g., penicillin, shellfish), reaction types, and severity.

Order confirmatory and severity/complication labs: repeat total Ca with albumin or ionized Ca, intact PTH, phosphorus, creatinine/eGFR, 25-OH vitamin D, magnesium, alkaline phosphatase; consider 24-hour urine calcium/creatinine (stone risk/FHH differentiation), and urinary calcium if not already available.
Interpret labs to confirm primary hyperparathyroidism (high Ca with non-suppressed/elevated PTH) and assess kidney function, vitamin D status, and complication risk.

State diagnosis as primary hyperparathyroidism due to primary parathyroid hyperplasia (multigland disease) and document supporting evidence; note any complications (stones, CKD, osteoporosis) if present.

Explain diagnosis, natural history, complications, and red flags; provide lifestyle guidance (hydration, avoid thiazides/lithium/excess calcium supplements, maintain normal dietary calcium) and monitoring needs.

Required safety check before prescribing; evaluate interactions for intended meds (e.g., cinacalcet with CYP inhibitors/QT-prolonging agents; vitamin D/calcium with hypercalcemia risk) and current medication list.
If a medication is planned, ensure interaction screen explicitly completed immediately before PRESCRIBE (can be the same step if performed after finalizing med choice).

If not immediate surgery or while awaiting surgery: consider cinacalcet for hypercalcemia control; cautiously replete vitamin D if deficient with close calcium monitoring; avoid inappropriate calcium supplementation unless post-op or hypocalcemia. Provide clear dosing/monitoring instructions.

If prescribing cinacalcet or vitamin D: order follow-up calcium, phosphorus, PTH (as appropriate), and renal function within a defined interval; consider ECG if QT risk or significant electrolyte abnormalities.

Arrange endocrine surgery referral for definitive management (subtotal or total parathyroidectomy with autotransplantation) and set follow-up timeline; include post-op monitoring plan for hypocalcemia/hungry bone syndrome.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, shared decision-making, and safety-net instructions.

Agent completes required safety checks: allergy_check before prescribing; contraindication_check before prescribing; drug_interaction_check before prescribing; contraindication_check before ordering.

Agent avoids unsafe recommendations: does not advise eliminating dietary calcium entirely; does not recommend thiazides or lithium; does not prescribe vitamin D or calcium without acknowledging hypercalcemia risk and a monitoring plan.
Agent explains definitive treatment is surgery for multigland hyperplasia and clearly distinguishes medical therapy (cinacalcet) as calcium-lowering but non-curative.
Agent provides post-operative counseling: risk of transient hypocalcemia/hungry bone syndrome, likely need for calcium/vitamin D supplementation, and lab monitoring schedule.
Agent addresses patient concerns and uses shared decision-making, incorporating symptoms, labs, kidney/bone findings, and patient preferences into the plan.
Agent reviews penicillin and shellfish allergies and reassures proposed meds are not related while still advising monitoring for any reaction.

Agent provides red-flag return precautions for severe hypercalcemia symptoms (confusion, vomiting, severe weakness, dehydration), arrhythmia symptoms, and severe constipation, and advises urgent evaluation when present.

minimal_paths.description: Confirms diagnosis and multigland hyperplasia implications, assesses complications, performs safety checks, counsels on definitive surgery with appropriate interim management and monitoring.
steps: ORDER_LAB (Ca/albumin or ionized Ca, PTH, phosphorus, Cr/eGFR, 25-OH D, Mg, Alk Phos, +/- 24h urine Ca/Cr)
DIAGNOSE (primary hyperparathyroidism due to parathyroid hyperplasia; note complications if present)
EDUCATE (diagnosis, complications, red flags, lifestyle, follow-up importance)
PRESCRIBE (as needed: cinacalcet and/or cautious vitamin D repletion with monitoring; avoid inappropriate calcium)
ORDER_LAB (monitor calcium/renal function after med initiation and pre-op planning)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (endocrine surgery referral; post-op hypocalcemia/hungry bone plan)

minimal_paths.description: Confirms diagnosis, provides counseling and referral for definitive surgery without initiating medications; still completes required ordering contraindication check and safety-net instructions.
steps: EDUCATE (include surgery definitive, lifestyle, red flags, monitoring)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (endocrine surgery referral and monitoring plan)

acceptable_variants.description: Orders additional evaluation for complications and differential (DEXA, renal imaging, 24-hour urine calcium) before finalizing treatment plan; still provides initial counseling and referral planning.
steps: ORDER_LAB (or equivalent ordering action for DEXA and renal imaging if supported by benchmark)

acceptable_variants.description: Initiates cinacalcet as bridge to surgery for symptomatic hypercalcemia, with explicit contraindication and interaction checks, and tight lab follow-up.
steps: ORDER_LAB (repeat Ca within 1-2 weeks or per protocol; renal function/electrolytes)

acceptable_variants.description: Addresses vitamin D deficiency with cautious repletion while monitoring calcium, emphasizing it is not curative and surgery remains definitive for hyperplasia.
steps: PRESCRIBE (vitamin D repletion with explicit monitoring plan and thresholds to hold/adjust)
ORDER_LAB (repeat Ca and 25-OH D at defined interval)
